name: Systems Programming
description: Operating systems, networking, protocols, and low-level programming
axioms: Everything is a file (Unix philosophy): devices, sockets, pipes are file descriptors
Memory is just bytes: all abstractions (types, structs, objects) are interpretation
Concurrency requires synchronization: races, deadlocks, livelocks are inevitable without it
Network is unreliable: design for latency, packet loss, and partial failure
Premature optimization is the root of all evil (Knuth)
Abstraction has cost: every layer adds latency and complexity
reasoning_patterns: Strace/ltrace: trace system calls and library calls to understand behavior
Packet capture: tcpdump/Wireshark to debug network issues
Memory debugging: Valgrind, AddressSanitizer, gdb for memory corruption
Performance profiling: perf, flame graphs to find bottlenecks
Race condition analysis: identify shared state, lock ordering, atomicity
boundary_conditions: Does not cover embedded systems in depth
Does not cover kernel development (device drivers, scheduler) in detail
key_references: W. Richard Stevens. Advanced Programming in the UNIX Environment
Kerrisk, M. The Linux Programming Interface
canonical_truths: TCP is reliable, ordered, connection-oriented; UDP is unreliable, unordered, connectionless
Context switch cost: ~1-10 μs (depends on cache state)
Memory-mapped I/O avoids kernel buffer copies
The C language has no bounds checking — buffer overflows are a C problem
